title: Chapter 20: Locked Doors of Mercy
paragraphs: The ash stopped falling.

It was not a mercy of nature, but a physical omen of catastrophe. Dawn never came. The air under the walls of Etcha suddenly became thick, viscous, and icy, as if the blood had stopped in the veins of the universe itself.
Rene, sitting by the cooled campfire, was the first to understand what was happening. The thin trickle of mint smoke from her cigarette stopped rising. It froze in the air as a motionless line.
The fox slowly rose to her feet. A primal horror was reflected in her yellow eyes.
"Wake up," she commanded quietly. "Time's up."

Yashil raised his head to the sky. He saw a pulsing vortex tear through the heavens. It was a Rift—a point where reality itself surrendered under the pressure of external darkness. Creatures that had no place in this world began to spill from the breach.

The gate towers of Etcha burst into a panicked clang of silver gongs. In an instant, the 'Grey City' turned into a boiling cauldron of despair. Thousands of beings whom Yashil had taught to live in a fragile peace over the last five years now rushed to the Great Gates. Dwarves, hydrols, runaway soldiers—all sought salvation from those who for years had looked down on them.

Yashil saw them all. He knew almost every face in this crowd. He had delivered babies in these tents, he had closed the eyes of the dying, he had judged their disputes. These were not 'refugees'—they were his people.
The captain of the guard appeared on the balcony. His face was whiter than marble. He looked down, and his hands were shaking.

"Forgive us..." the captain whispered. He abruptly waved his hand. The small wickets of the Great Gates slammed shut with a clang. The jade walls flared—Etcha's magical shield closed from the inside, leaving thousands of souls outside.
The crowd at the gate froze. The screams died down. It was a silence of absolute hopelessness. They realized they had been betrayed by those they considered superior beings.
The creatures from the Rift began to land. They looked only at the defenseless crowd.
Rene gripped Yashil firmly by the shoulder.
"We need to leave. I know a trail in the ravines. There's nothing we can do, Yashil. This is a slaughter. Let's go!"
Dzhanavar shifted heavily from paw to paw, gripping his axe.
"Wolves don't run, little fox. But we can't shield everyone. Green... it's time to go."

Yashil looked at the old dwarf whose cart he had fixed a month ago. At the woman with the child he had treated for fever. For five years, he had been their quiet voice. He could not become their silence now.
The goblin gently pushed Rene's hand from his shoulder.

"Leave," Yashil said quietly. There was no fear in his voice. "You are my friends. But these people have believed for seven years that kindness is worth something. If I leave now, I will deceive them more than the elves did."
The little goblin picked up the heavy iron pipe from the ground. He stood alone in the middle of the tract, between thousands of defenseless people and the advancing nightmare.
Dzhanavar looked at his back for a long time. Then he drove his axe into the stone next to Yashil with a crash.

"For seven years you've been poisoning me with your compassion, little one..." the werewolf growled, his muscles beginning to fill with primal power. "Now don't complain. You don't abandon the pack. Auf!"
Rene let out a shaky breath and drew two daggers.
"My corset will be hopelessly ruined in this mess," she hissed coldly, standing to the left. "But I won't let them devour the only one who read me poetry in this hell."
Kirah stepped forward, shielding them all with his colossal body.
